text: The recent boom in location-based systems and the ease with which an unknown/untrusted server can gather a huge amount of personal location data from users has raised a number of privacy concerns. In this work, we study the geo-indistinguishability privacy definition for location-based systems. The geo-indistinguishability notion provides a framework that protects a user’s true location while allowing an approximation of the location to be published. The latter is necessary to get the desired service. The geo-indistinguishability definition provides protection for the user location up to some radius r, with a privacy level that depends on r. This definition is a generalized version of the differential privacy definition. We present a perturbation mechanism for achieving the geo-indistinguishability by adding appropriate noise to the user location. We show a case study for an LBS application that illustrates the usability of our privacy mechanism. We also compare our mechanism with other known mechanisms in the literature and show that our mechanism achieves the highest level of privacy, for the same level of utility, in comparison with mechanisms that are not based on prior distribution.
